guides.guideDescription: Guidelines developed by the European Association of Science Editors (EASE) to improve clarity, completeness, and international readability of scientific manuscripts written in English.

analysis.item_question: Is the title unambiguous, specific, and reflective of the article content?
analysis.item_question_background: Vague or ambiguous titles impair discoverability and may mislead readers about the study scope, increasing citation distortion and misclassification in indexing databases.
analysis.comment: The title is specific and clearly reflects the study content, identifying the intervention comparison, target population, condition, and trial name.
quotes: Combined Exercise Training vs Health Education for Older Adults with Hypertension: The HAEL Randomized Clinical Trial

analysis.item_question: If relevant, does the title include study period, location, organism scientific name, or experimental design?
analysis.item_question_background: Omitting contextual identifiers such as location, species, or design reduces reproducibility and prevents accurate interpretation of study generalizability.
analysis.comment: The title includes explicit experimental design context via 'Randomized Clinical Trial.' It does not include period or location, but those are only relevant if needed.
quotes: Combined Exercise Training vs Health Education for Older Adults with Hypertension: The HAEL Randomized Clinical Trial

analysis.item_question: If human subjects of only one sex were included, is this specified in the title?
analysis.item_question_background: Failure to disclose sex-restricted sampling obscures biological applicability and may contribute to sex-based bias in interpretation and downstream clinical translation.
analysis.comment: This study did not include only one sex; the manuscript reports both female and male participants, so sex-specific wording in the title is not applicable.

analysis.item_question: Are all authors listed with affiliations, present correspondence address, and email addresses?
analysis.item_question_background: Incomplete author metadata prevents accountability, inhibits correspondence, and complicates institutional attribution and research integrity verification.
analysis.comment: The manuscript lists all authors with affiliations and provides a corresponding author postal address and email, but it does not provide email addresses for all authors.
quotes: Affiliations: 1 Universidade Federal do Rio Grande do Sul (UFRGS), Porto Alegre, Brazil 2 Universidade de São Paulo (USP), São Paulo, Brazil 3 Universidade Federal do Ceará, Fortaleza, Brazil 4 Universidade Federal de Pelotas, Pelotas, Brazil 5 Northumbria University, Newcastle upon Tyne, United Kingdom 6 University of Vienna, Vienna, Austria 7 Hospital de Clínicas de Porto Alegre (HCPA), Porto Alegre, Brazil 8 The University of Texas at Austin, Austin, Texas, USA 9 Universidade Federal do Rio Grande, Rio Grande, Brazil 10 Universidade Federal do Rio Grande do Norte, Natal, Brazil 11 University of Connecticut, Storrs, Connecticut, USA
Corresponding author: Daniel Umpierre, ScD Rua Ramiro Barcelos 2350 Hospital de Clinicas de Porto Alegre (HCPA), Centro de Pesquisa Clínica 21302 Porto Alegre, RS, Brazil danielumpierre@gmail.com

analysis.item_question: Are ORCiD identifiers provided for authors (encouraged)?
analysis.item_question_background: Without persistent digital identifiers, author disambiguation becomes unreliable, leading to attribution errors in citation tracking systems.
analysis.comment: I could not locate supporting evidence in the manuscript content provided for this request that ORCiD identifiers were provided.

analysis.item_question: Does the abstract contain background, objectives, methods, results, and conclusions?
analysis.item_question_background: Structured abstracts reduce selective outcome emphasis and improve indexing accuracy by ensuring balanced representation of study rationale, execution, and findings.
analysis.comment: The abstract is structured and includes background/rationale, objective, methods, results, and conclusions.
quotes: Objective: To determine whether a pragmatic combined exercise training program is more effective than a health education program in reducing ambulatory BP in older adults with hypertension.

Design: The “Hypertension Approaches in the Elderly: a Lifestyle study” (HAEL) was a multicenter, single-blinded, randomized clinical trial. Results: Out of 717 individuals screened, 160 participants (mean [SD] age, 67.4 [5.5]; 104 female [62%]) were randomized. Conclusions and Relevance: Among participants in the HAEL study over 3 months, combined exercise training was not superior to a health education program to reduce ambulatory BP.

analysis.item_question: Does the abstract avoid references to tables or figures?
analysis.item_question_background: Abstracts are often indexed independently; referencing tables or figures creates inaccessible claims when separated from the full manuscript.
analysis.comment: The abstract avoids references to tables or figures.
analysis.quote_verification_note: This item is satisfied because the manuscript avoids prohibited wording, so there may be no supporting quote.

analysis.item_question: Does the abstract avoid literature references unless absolutely necessary and fully detailed?
analysis.item_question_background: Citations in abstracts can mislead indexing systems and create incomplete bibliographic records when abstracts are distributed without reference lists.
analysis.comment: The abstract avoids literature references/citation markers.
analysis.quote_verification_note: This item is satisfied because the manuscript avoids prohibited wording, so there may be no supporting quote.

analysis.item_question: Is all information presented in the abstract also reported in the main body?
analysis.item_question_background: Discrepancies between abstract and body may constitute selective reporting and distort reader interpretation or meta-analytic extraction.
analysis.comment: Key abstract information is reported in the main body, including trial design, participant number, primary findings on ambulatory blood pressure, secondary functional findings, and the overall conclusion.
quotes: This randomized, single-blinded, multicenter, two-arm, parallel superiority trial was conducted in two cities in southern Brazil: Porto Alegre (coordinating center) and Pelotas.

No significant between-group differences were observed for systolic or diastolic ambulatory BP across any timeframe (24-hour, daytime, or nighttime; all interaction P ≥ 0.57). For VO 2 peak, exercise training showed greater improvement than health education (exercise training: +0.4 ml/kg/min, 95% CI, −0.5 to 1.3; health education: −1.7 ml/kg/min, 95% CI −2.6 to −0.8; between-group difference: 2.1 ml/kg/min; interaction P=0.01). Combined exercise training was not superior to health education for reducing ambulatory or office BP in older adults with hypertension, despite improving cardiorespiratory fitness and lower limb function.

analysis.item_question: Do keywords include relevant scientific terms not already in the title?
analysis.item_question_background: Redundant keywords reduce indexing breadth and may limit discoverability across thematic databases.
analysis.comment: I could not locate supporting evidence in the manuscript content provided for this request of any visible 'Keywords' or 'Keywords:' section in the abstract-associated front matter, so I could not verify whether keywords include relevant scientific terms not already in the title.

analysis.item_question: Are the keywords specific rather than general?
analysis.item_question_background: Overly broad keywords dilute search precision and impair targeted retrieval in bibliographic databases.
analysis.comment: Skipped automatically because prerequisite item was not found.

analysis.item_question: Are broader interdisciplinary terms included when appropriate?
analysis.item_question_background: Failure to include cross-disciplinary indexing terms can isolate research from adjacent fields where findings may have translational relevance.
analysis.comment: Skipped automatically because prerequisite item was not found.

guides.guideDescription: Guidance developed by the European Association of Science Editors (EASE) to improve clarity, transparency, ethical compliance, and research integrity in scientific manuscripts structured according to the IMRaD format.

analysis.item_question: Does the Introduction clearly explain why the study was needed?
analysis.item_question_background: Without a clearly stated rationale, readers cannot assess the scientific gap being addressed, which undermines the justification for the study and weakens its contribution to the field.
analysis.comment: The Introduction clearly explains the study need by outlining the high burden of hypertension in older adults, low physical activity uptake, and the evidence gap for pragmatic trials using ambulatory blood pressure monitoring in this population.
quotes: Hypertension affects over 60% of adults aged 55 years or older 1 , yet only 36% of older adults with hypertension meet physical activity guidelines 2 , despite exercise being an established treatment component 3 . This gap underscores the need for pragmatic, scalable interventions that integrate into healthcare systems while addressing the physiological changes and comorbidities of aging.

However, there is insufficient evidence derived from studies specifically designed for older adults, especially through pragmatic interventions to facilitate implementation in public health settings 4 . Additionally, there is a scarcity of trials employing the gold standard measure of BP monitoring, ambulatory or 24-hour BP.

analysis.item_question: Are the research objectives or specific research questions clearly stated?
analysis.item_question_background: If research objectives are vague or absent, readers cannot determine what the study intends to answer, compromising interpretability and evaluation of results.
analysis.comment: The research objective is clearly stated at the end of the Introduction, along with the main comparison and outcomes framework.
quotes: We evaluated the effects of a 12-week combined exercise intervention on ambulatory BP in older adults with hypertension, compared with an active health education control group.

analysis.item_question: Does the Introduction move from general background to the specific research question?
analysis.item_question_background: A poorly structured introduction can obscure the logical development of the research problem, making it difficult to follow how the specific question emerges from broader context.
analysis.comment: The Introduction follows a logical progression from broad epidemiologic background, to limitations in existing evidence, to the specific study question and planned comparison.
quotes: Hypertension affects over 60% of adults aged 55 years or older 1 , yet only 36% of older adults with hypertension meet physical activity guidelines 2 , despite exercise being an established treatment component 3 .
We evaluated the effects of a 12-week combined exercise intervention on ambulatory BP in older adults with hypertension, compared with an active health education control group.

analysis.item_question: If applicable, is the tested hypothesis explicitly formulated?
analysis.item_question_background: Failure to state a hypothesis in hypothesis-driven research obscures the analytical framework and limits the reader’s ability to evaluate whether conclusions align with prespecified expectations.
analysis.comment: An explicit hypothesis is stated in the Introduction.
quotes: We hypothesized that exercise would produce greater reductions in systolic ambulatory BP, and assessed secondary outcomes related to cardiovascular health and physical function.

analysis.item_question: Does the Methods section describe in sufficient detail how the study was carried out?
analysis.item_question_background: Insufficient methodological detail prevents reproducibility and limits the ability of other researchers to verify or replicate the findings.
analysis.comment: The Methods section gives substantial procedural detail on study design, setting, participant eligibility, randomization/blinding, interventions, outcomes, measurement tools, statistical analysis, and protocol deviations, sufficient to understand how the study was carried out.
quotes: This randomized, single-blinded, multicenter, two-arm, parallel superiority trial was conducted in two cities in southern Brazil: Porto Alegre (coordinating center) and Pelotas. The trial was registered at ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT03264443), and the full protocol was previously published6.

Randomization followed a 1:1 ratio with permuted blocks of random sizes, allocating participants to combined exercise training or health education. The allocation sequence was generated electronically and maintained by a researcher not involved in interventions or data collection. Outcome assessors were blinded to participant allocation.

analysis.item_question: Are all factors that could have affected the results considered?
analysis.item_question_background: Unreported confounding or influencing factors can introduce bias and mislead interpretation of the study’s findings.
analysis.comment: The manuscript considers several factors that could have affected results, including adherence, protocol deviations, early termination, missing quality-of-life data, and possible medication changes, but it does not comprehensively control or report all such factors; for example, antihypertensive medication changes were not systematically monitored.
quotes: The protocol had three main deviations. First, the Pelotas center closed due to low recruitment, affecting approximately half of its planned target. The coordinating center assumed remaining recruitment responsibilities. Second, the trial was terminated early due to COVID-19 restrictions in June 2020. After consulting external advisors, the coordinating committee ended the trial with 168 participants randomized and 160 completed. Third, the quality-of-life questionnaire was changed to the SF-36 mid-study to enable economic analyses, resulting in missing data for approximately 60 participants.

First, we did not systematically monitor changes in antihypertensive medication during the trial. Although we expect this was infrequent, this lack of specific control limit exploratory analysis of treatment responders.

analysis.item_question: If experimental materials were obtained from biobanks, are full names and identifiers reported?
analysis.item_question_background: Failure to report biobank sources and identifiers impedes traceability of materials and may compromise reproducibility and ethical oversight.
analysis.comment: No biobank-derived experimental materials were identified, and I could not locate supporting evidence in the manuscript content provided for this request.

analysis.item_question: If citing non-English or inaccessible methods, are they fully explained?
analysis.item_question_background: Relying on inaccessible sources without detailed explanation prevents verification and limits accessibility for international readers.
analysis.comment: Methods that also reference external sources or publicly accessible files are still described in sufficient procedural detail within the Methods section, so the manuscript does not rely solely on inaccessible or non-English sources for essential procedures.
quotes: The participants allocated to combined exercise training intervention completed a supervised exercise program with individual sessions lasting approximately 1 hour three times a week. All sessions consisted of cardiorespiratory and muscular strengthening exercises and were structured with an initial warm-up (<5 min), followed by 20–30 min of aerobic exercise at moderate intensity and 2–3 sets of 4–5 multi-joint resistance exercises, emphasizing major muscle groups (lasting 15-20 min), and 5–10 min of cool-down.

The primary outcome was systolic ambulatory BP measured on the non-dominant arm for 24 hours with an automatic portable device (90207, SpaceLabs, Redmond, WA, USA). The final assessment was conducted at least 24 hours after the last intervention session and within 10 days after the participant completed the training intervention.

analysis.item_question: Does the manuscript confirm compliance with relevant ethical standards?
analysis.item_question_background: Failure to report ethical compliance raises concerns about participant protection, animal welfare, or environmental harm, potentially invalidating the study.
analysis.comment: I could not locate a statement confirming ethics committee/institutional review board approval, informed consent, or explicit compliance with ethical standards such as the Declaration of Helsinki in the manuscript content provided for this request.

analysis.item_question: Does the Results section present only new findings from the study?
analysis.item_question_background: Including previously published data in the Results section can confuse the originality of findings and distort the distinction between background and novel contributions.
analysis.comment: The Results section mainly reports study findings, but it also includes adherence figures and a statement pointing readers to a previously published recruitment flow, which introduces non-outcome/process material rather than only new findings.
quotes: A total of 168 participants were enrolled in the study. Eight participants were unable to finish the protocol. Therefore, data on 160 participants were analyzed. A flow diagram detailing the trial recruitment process is presented in Figure 1.
Adherence to the interventions was 52.5% for the combined exercise training group and 70.7% for the health education program in the intention-to-treat analysis.

analysis.item_name: Tables and Figures Referenced and Ordered
analysis.item_question: Are all tables and figures mentioned in the text and numbered sequentially?
analysis.item_question_background: Improperly referenced or misnumbered tables and figures reduce clarity and hinder the reader’s ability to verify reported findings.
analysis.comment: All detected display items are mentioned in the text, and the numbering is sequential: Figure 1, then Tables 1 through 3.
quotes: A flow diagram detailing the trial recruitment process is presented in Figure 1.

The majority of the sample consisted of women, individuals identifying as white, and participants with a body mass index marginally within the obesity range ( Table 1 ). On average, BP was within the limits of hypertension control... All estimated marginal means and p-values for interaction terms and pre-post post-hoc tests derived from the ITT dataset are presented in Table 2 . ... No significant between-group differences were observed for flow-mediated dilatation at the mid-intervention and end-of-intervention assessments (Table 3).

analysis.item_question: Is the statistical analysis appropriate and correctly applied?
analysis.item_question_background: Inappropriate statistical methods can lead to incorrect conclusions and invalidate the study’s findings.
analysis.comment: The manuscript describes analyses that match the randomized parallel-group design and repeated pre/post measurements, including baseline adjustment, intention-to-treat and prespecified per-protocol analyses, confidence intervals, multiplicity-adjusted post hoc tests, and a defined significance threshold.
quotes: Generalized estimated equations with an independence model as the covariance matrix and adjusted for baseline values were calculated for each variable of interest, with time and allocation group as factors. Post-hoc analyses were conducted, whenever applicable, using the Tukey test adjusted for multiple comparisons.

Datasets were divided in intention-to-treat (ITT) - where all randomized individuals with at least one measurement (irrespective of adherence and completion status) were considered - and per protocol - where only non-dropout and adherent ( ≥ 70%) subjects were considered. Data are presented as estimated marginal means with 95% confidence intervals or as otherwise stated. Statistical significance was defined as a two-sided P<.05 for the primary and secondary outcomes.

analysis.item_question: Are data disaggregated by sex when involving humans or animals?
analysis.item_question_background: Aggregating data without sex disaggregation can obscure biologically meaningful differences and limit the generalizability of findings.
analysis.comment: The manuscript reports baseline sex distribution, but the Results are not disaggregated or stratified by sex for outcomes.
quotes: The mean age for both groups was approximately 67 ± 5.5 years. The majority of the sample consisted of women...

analysis.item_question: Is there evidence that data are complete, not fabricated, distorted, or selectively excluded?
analysis.item_question_background: Data fabrication, selective omission, or image manipulation constitute scientific misconduct and fundamentally compromise research integrity.
analysis.comment: The manuscript presents multiple transparency features consistent with complete reporting, including participant flow, protocol deviations, intention-to-treat and per-protocol analyses, and tabulated outcomes. However, the text also reports early termination, center closure, and missing quality-of-life data for about 60 participants, which means completeness is not fully demonstrated and some selective exclusion risk remains mitigated but not eliminated.
quotes: The protocol had three main deviations. First, the Pelotas center closed due to low recruitment, affecting approximately half of its planned target. The coordinating center assumed remaining recruitment responsibilities. Second, the trial was terminated early due to COVID-19 restrictions in June 2020. After consulting external advisors, the coordinating committee ended the trial with 168 participants randomized and 160 completed. Third, the quality-of-life questionnaire was changed to the SF-36 mid-study to enable economic analyses, resulting in missing data for approximately 60 participants.

Generalized estimated equations with an independence model as the covariance matrix and adjusted for baseline values were calculated for each variable of interest, with time and allocation group as factors... Datasets were divided in intention-to-treat (ITT) - where all randomized individuals with at least one measurement (irrespective of adherence and completion status) were considered - and per protocol - where only non-dropout and adherent ( ≥ 70%) subjects were considered.

analysis.item_question: Does the Discussion avoid introducing new results?
analysis.item_question_background: Introducing new data in the Discussion blurs structural boundaries and prevents proper scrutiny of results presentation.
analysis.comment: The Discussion largely interprets previously reported findings, but it also introduces a specific numerical result for office systolic blood pressure that is not clearly first presented in the Results narrative in the same detail and thus functions as new result-level reporting within Discussion.
quotes: Discussion Contrary to our hypothesis, combined exercise training did not reduce ambulatory BP beyond the health education program.

In the office BP analysis, we observed a significant within-group reduction in the exercise training group, which presented a 5.4- mmHg reduction in systolic BP after the intervention (from 135.1 mmHg [95% CI, 134.0 – 136.1] to 129.7 mmHg [95% CI, 126.2 - 133.1]).

analysis.item_question: Does the Discussion explicitly answer the research questions?
analysis.item_question_background: Failing to explicitly connect findings to the stated research questions weakens the logical coherence of the manuscript.
analysis.comment: The Discussion explicitly answers the study question by stating that combined exercise training did not reduce ambulatory blood pressure more than health education, directly addressing the stated objective and hypothesis.
quotes: Objective: To determine whether a pragmatic combined exercise training program is more effective than a health education program in reducing ambulatory BP in older adults with hypertension.
Discussion Contrary to our hypothesis, combined exercise training did not reduce ambulatory BP beyond the health education program.

analysis.item_question: Are the main results objectively compared with published data?
analysis.item_question_background: Without contextual comparison, readers cannot evaluate how the findings align with or diverge from existing evidence.
analysis.comment: The Discussion compares the main findings with published evidence and explains how this trial differs methodologically from prior studies, including citations to meta-analyses and earlier trials.
quotes: Although these findings differ from current evidence on exercise effectiveness for BP reduction, key methodological features distinguish the HAEL study from prior trials 17 .
Therefore, our results do not conflict with existing meta-analyses estimates that mostly derived from office BP measurements 18–20 .

analysis.item_question: Are study limitations discussed?
analysis.item_question_background: Failure to disclose limitations may overstate confidence in findings and mislead readers regarding their reliability.
analysis.comment: The manuscript includes a dedicated limitations discussion that explicitly acknowledges several study weaknesses and constraints.
quotes: Strengths and limitations Some limitations should be considered in our study. First, we did not systematically monitor changes in antihypertensive medication during the trial.
Second, the early termination of the study due to the COVID-19 pandemic may have affected the statistical power of our analyses.

analysis.item_question: Are the main findings clearly highlighted?
analysis.item_question_background: If key findings are not emphasized, the study’s primary contribution may be obscured.
analysis.comment: The Discussion clearly highlights the main findings at the outset and reiterates the key outcome patterns, including the null primary blood pressure result and selected functional improvements.
quotes: Contrary to our hypothesis, combined exercise training did not reduce ambulatory BP beyond the health education program.
Exercise training improved VO2peak and SPPB scores but not six-minute walk distance, highlighting potential cardiovascular and disability risk reductions regardless of BP effects.

analysis.item_question: If the study included only one sex, are implications for generalizability discussed?
analysis.item_question_background: Failing to discuss single-sex sampling limits transparency regarding external validity across sexes.
analysis.comment: This item does not apply because the study did not include only one sex; both female and male participants were enrolled.
quotes: Out of 717 individuals screened, 160 participants (mean [SD] age, 67.4 [5.5]; 104 female [62%]) were randomized.

analysis.item_question: Are findings contrary to the authors’ viewpoint considered?
analysis.item_question_background: Ignoring contradictory evidence introduces confirmation bias and reduces the credibility of interpretations.
analysis.comment: The discussion explicitly considers findings that conflict with the authors’ hypothesis and with prior evidence on exercise reducing blood pressure.
quotes: Contrary to our hypothesis, combined exercise training did not reduce ambulatory BP beyond the health education program.
Although these findings differ from current evidence on exercise effectiveness for BP reduction, key methodological features distinguish the HAEL study from prior trials

analysis.item_question: Are major conclusions and practical significance clearly emphasized?
analysis.item_question_background: Without a clear conclusion and statement of practical relevance, readers may struggle to understand the study’s real-world or theoretical impact.
analysis.comment: The manuscript clearly emphasizes the main conclusion and practical significance in the concluding discussion/conclusion text, including implications for non-pharmacological programs and the complexity of BP control in this population.
quotes: Combined exercise training was not superior to health education for reducing ambulatory or office BP in older adults with hypertension, despite improving cardiorespiratory fitness and lower limb function.

Both interventions improved quality of life, highlighting the value of non-pharmacological programs. These findings underscore the complex relationships between exercise, BP control, and antihypertensive treatment in this population.

guides.guideDescription: Merged declaration-focused guide combining EASE recommendations for acknowledgments, funding transparency, permissions, and conflict-of-interest disclosures.

analysis.item_question: Are all sources of funding acknowledged?
analysis.item_question_background: Incomplete funding disclosure may conceal financial influences that could bias study design, analysis, or reporting.
analysis.comment: The manuscript explicitly acknowledges multiple funding sources in a dedicated Funding section.
quotes: Funding Funded by CNPq grant number 429849/2016–8 (Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico), Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior–Brazil (CAPES) - Finance Code 001, FAPERGS grant number 17/2551-0000515-5 (Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado do Rio Grande do Sul) and FIPE-HCPA project number 2017-0044.

analysis.item_question: Is the funding statement presented in the recommended standardized format?
analysis.item_question_background: Non-standardized funding statements may reduce clarity and hinder indexing or auditing of research support.
analysis.comment: The funding statement is presented in a structured Funding section and includes named funders with grant or project identifiers.
quotes: Funding Funded by CNPq grant number 429849/2016–8 (Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico), Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior–Brazil (CAPES) - Finance Code 001, FAPERGS grant number 17/2551-0000515-5 (Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado do Rio Grande do Sul) and FIPE-HCPA project number 2017-0044.

analysis.item_question: If funding is present, is it described with clear wording (funder name and grant number), or, if no funding, is this explicitly stated?
analysis.item_question_background: EASE provides model funding language, including a standard sentence when no specific grant was provided, to promote clarity and transparency.
analysis.comment: Funding is described clearly with funder names and corresponding grant, finance code, or project numbers.
quotes: Funded by CNPq grant number 429849/2016–8 (Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico), Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior–Brazil (CAPES) - Finance Code 001, FAPERGS grant number 17/2551-0000515-5 (Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado do Rio Grande do Sul) and FIPE-HCPA project number 2017-0044.

analysis.item_question: If no funding was received, is an explicit no-funding statement included?
analysis.item_question_background: Omitting a no-funding declaration may create ambiguity about undisclosed financial support.
analysis.comment: This item does not apply because the manuscript reports that funding was received rather than stating that no funding was received.
quotes: Funded by CNPq grant number 429849/2016–8 (Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico), Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior–Brazil (CAPES) - Finance Code 001, FAPERGS grant number 17/2551-0000515-5 (Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado do Rio Grande do Sul) and FIPE-HCPA project number 2017-0044.

analysis.item_question: Are other relevant financial or personal conflicts of interest disclosed?
analysis.item_question_background: Undisclosed personal or financial relationships may introduce hidden bias and undermine trust in the findings.
analysis.comment: The manuscript discloses other relevant financial or personal conflicts of interest by stating that none exist.
quotes: Disclosures None of the authors disclose potential conflicts of interest.

analysis.item_question: Do the authors explicitly declare whether conflicts of interest exist, either stating that none exist or describing any potential conflicts?
analysis.item_question_background: The EASE ethics checklist and related form require authors to declare that there is no conflict of interest or to describe circumstances that could constitute a potential conflict.
analysis.comment: The authors explicitly declare that no conflicts of interest exist.
quotes: Disclosures None of the authors disclose potential conflicts of interest.

analysis.item_question: Is any declared conflict of interest clearly disclosed either in the acknowledgments or in a separate ‘Conflicts of Interest’ section, as EASE suggests?
analysis.item_question_background: EASE notes that conflicts may be mentioned in the acknowledgments or in a dedicated conflicts-of-interest section following acknowledgments.
analysis.comment: The conflict-of-interest disclosure is clearly presented in a separate labeled section following the acknowledgements.
quotes: Acknowledgements The authors would like to acknowledge the role of Angelica Zanotto, SME-PMPA, and GPPG-HCPA staff members in supporting this research. Special thanks are given to individuals who have volunteered for this project.
Disclosures None of the authors disclose potential conflicts of interest.

analysis.item_question: If previously published materials are reproduced, is permission obtained and acknowledged?
analysis.item_question_background: Failure to secure and acknowledge copyright permissions can result in legal infringement and ethical breaches.
analysis.comment: I could not locate supporting evidence in the manuscript content provided for this request that previously published materials were reproduced with a permission acknowledgment.

analysis.item_question: Is assistance from language professionals, statisticians, or data collectors acknowledged transparently?
analysis.item_question_background: Undisclosed editorial or analytical assistance may obscure external influence on data interpretation or manuscript preparation.
analysis.comment: The acknowledgements transparently mention supporting staff, but I could not locate explicit acknowledgment of language professionals, statisticians, or data collectors by role, nor a statement assigning final manuscript responsibility to the authors.
quotes: Acknowledgements The authors would like to acknowledge the role of Angelica Zanotto, SME-PMPA, and GPPG-HCPA staff members in supporting this research. Special thanks are given to individuals who have volunteered for this project.

analysis.item_question: Are all substantial contributors who do not qualify as co-authors properly acknowledged?
analysis.item_question_background: Failing to acknowledge substantial non-author contributors can obscure intellectual or technical contributions and distort accountability for the work.
analysis.comment: The manuscript acknowledges non-author contributors in the Acknowledgements section. I could not verify from the manuscript alone whether these are all substantial non-author contributors, but explicit acknowledgments are present.
quotes: Acknowledgements The authors would like to acknowledge the role of Angelica Zanotto, SME-PMPA, and GPPG-HCPA staff members in supporting this research.
Special thanks are given to individuals who have volunteered for this project.

analysis.item_question: Does the manuscript acknowledge all substantial non-author contributors and all sources of funding, as recommended by EASE?
analysis.item_question_background: EASE states that acknowledgments should mention all people who contributed substantially but are not co-authors, and that all sources of funding should be acknowledged.
analysis.comment: The manuscript includes both acknowledgments of non-author contributors and a funding statement listing multiple funding sources and identifiers.
quotes: Acknowledgements The authors would like to acknowledge the role of Angelica Zanotto, SME-PMPA, and GPPG-HCPA staff members in supporting this research. Special thanks are given to individuals who have volunteered for this project.

analysis.item_question: Do the authors declare that all acknowledged individuals agreed to be named, that consent was obtained for unpublished data, and that permissions were obtained for reused figures or tables?
analysis.item_question_background: The EASE ethics checklist requires authors to confirm consent from people named in acknowledgments, from authors of unpublished data, and from copyright owners of reused materials.
analysis.comment: I could not locate supporting evidence in the manuscript content provided for this request that acknowledged individuals agreed to be named, that consent was obtained for unpublished data, or that permissions were obtained for reused figures or tables.

guides.guideDescription: CONSORT 2025 provides an updated minimum set of reporting items for randomised trial reports, aiming to make methods and results clear, complete, and transparent. It includes a 30-item checklist (with sub-items) and related guidance to help authors, editors, and reviewers assess whether key information about design, conduct, analysis, and findings is reported.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 1a (Identification as a randomised trial) reported?
analysis.comment: The study is identified as a randomized clinical trial in the title.
quotes: Combined Exercise Training vs Health Education for Older Adults with Hypertension: The HAEL Randomized Clinical Trial

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 1b (Structured summary (design, methods, results, conclusions)) reported?
analysis.comment: The abstract is structured into sections covering importance, objective, design, setting, participants, interventions, main outcomes, results, and conclusions.
quotes: Design: The “Hypertension Approaches in the Elderly: a Lifestyle study” (HAEL) was a multicenter, single-blinded, randomized clinical trial.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 2 (Trial registration) reported?
analysis.comment: The trial registration is reported in both the abstract and the methods section.
quotes: Trial Registration: ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: NCT03264443.

analysis.item_name: Protocol and statistical analysis plan access
analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 3 (Protocol and statistical analysis plan access) reported?
analysis.comment: The manuscript references a previously published full protocol and provides a link to the analysis script.
quotes: The trial was registered at ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT03264443), and the full protocol was previously published 6.

analysis.item_name: Data sharing (participant data, code, materials) access
analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 4 (Data sharing (participant data, code, materials) access) reported?
analysis.comment: The authors provide a public link to the analysis script (code) and exercise protocol (materials), but the text does not explicitly state the availability or sharing status of the individual participant data.
quotes: Exercises employed during the intervention are documented and publicly accessible (https://osf.io/56wrv/files - Exercise protocol) 10.
The full script for the analysis is public available (https://osf.io/56wrv/files - Data analysis) 10.

analysis.item_name: Funding sources and role of funders
analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 5a (Funding sources and role of funders) reported?
analysis.comment: The manuscript lists several funding sources and grant numbers, but it does not specify the role of the funders in the design, conduct, or reporting of the study.
quotes: Funded by CNPq grant number 429849/2016–8 (Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico), Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior– Brazil (CAPES) - Finance Code 001, FAPERGS grant number 17/2551-0000515-5 (Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado do Rio Grande do Sul) and FIPE-HCPA project number 2017-0044.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 5b (Author conflicts of interest) reported?
analysis.comment: The authors explicitly state that they have no potential conflicts of interest to disclose.
quotes: None of the authors disclose potential conflicts of interest.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 6 (Scientific background and rationale) reported?
analysis.comment: The introduction provides a rationale by highlighting the prevalence of hypertension in older adults and the lack of evidence from pragmatic trials using ambulatory blood pressure monitoring in this specific population.
quotes: This gap underscores the need for pragmatic, scalable interventions that integrate into healthcare systems while addressing the physiological changes and comorbidities of aging.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 7 (Specific objectives (benefits and harms)) reported?
analysis.comment: The objectives are clearly stated at the end of the introduction, specifying the primary and secondary outcome evaluations.
quotes: We evaluated the effects of a 12-week combined exercise intervention on ambulatory BP in older adults with hypertension, compared with an active health education control group.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 8 (Patient and public involvement) reported?
analysis.comment: The authors explicitly state that patients and the public were not involved in the study.
quotes: Patients and the public were not involved in the design, conduct, or reporting of this trial.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 9 (Trial design) reported?
analysis.comment: The trial design is described as a randomized, single-blinded, multicenter, two-arm, parallel superiority trial.
quotes: This randomized, single-blinded, multicenter, two-arm, parallel superiority trial was conducted in two cities in southern Brazil: Porto Alegre (coordinating center) and Pelotas.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 10 (Changes to trial after commencement) reported?
analysis.comment: The manuscript describes three main protocol deviations, including site closure, early termination, and a questionnaire change.
quotes: The protocol had three main deviations. First, the Pelotas center closed due to low recruitment... Second, the trial was terminated early due to COVID-19 restrictions... Third, the quality-of-life questionnaire was changed to the SF-36 mid-study

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 11 (Trial setting) reported?
analysis.comment: The trial setting is identified as two cities in southern Brazil.
quotes: This randomized, single-blinded, multicenter, two-arm, parallel superiority trial was conducted in two cities in southern Brazil: Porto Alegre (coordinating center) and Pelotas.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 12a (Eligibility criteria for participants) reported?
analysis.comment: Inclusion and exclusion criteria for participants are detailed.
quotes: Eligible participants were: 1) individuals aged ≥ 60 years with hypertension... 2) stable pharmacological regimen... 3) willingness to participate... Exclusion criteria included recent cardiovascular events (<6 months), chronic heart failure (NYHA class III or IV), unstable arrhythmia

analysis.item_name: Eligibility criteria for sites and intervention deliverers (if applicable)
analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 12b (Eligibility criteria for sites and intervention deliverers (if applicable)) reported?
analysis.comment: The manuscript identifies the types of deliverers (e.g., trained health professionals, exercise physiologists) but does not explicitly state the eligibility criteria for their selection or the criteria for site selection.
quotes: The education program was conducted in groups and was focused on standardized content delivered interactively by trained health professionals
Oxygen consumption... were recorded breath-by-breath during an incremental protocol supervised by exercise physiologists and physicians.

analysis.item_name: Intervention and comparator details (replication; materials access if relevant)
analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 13 (Intervention and comparator details (replication; materials access if relevant)) reported?
analysis.comment: Intervention details for both groups are provided, including structure, intensity monitoring, and links to the full protocol and exercise materials.
quotes: All sessions consisted of cardiorespiratory and muscular strengthening exercises and were structured with an initial warm-up (<5 min), followed by 20–30 min of aerobic exercise at moderate intensity and 2–3 sets of 4–5 multi-joint resistance exercises
Exercises employed during the intervention are documented and publicly accessible (https://osf.io/56wrv/files - Exercise protocol)

analysis.item_name: Pre-specified outcomes (measurement variable, metric, aggregation, timepoint)
analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 14 (Pre-specified outcomes (measurement variable, metric, aggregation, timepoint)) reported?
analysis.comment: Primary and secondary outcomes are defined with measurement methods and timepoints.
quotes: The primary outcome was systolic ambulatory BP measured on the non-dominant arm for 24 hours with an automatic portable device (90207, SpaceLabs, Redmond, WA, USA).
Secondary outcomes were diastolic BP, endothelial function, functional capacity, and quality of life.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 15 (Harms definition and assessment methods) reported?
analysis.comment: Assessment of adverse events was conducted via a weekly survey, though a formal definition of harms is not explicitly provided in the methods.
quotes: All participants answered a weekly survey on pharmacological treatment changes, adverse events, and exercise performed outside of the study.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 16a (Sample size determination) reported?
analysis.comment: The sample size calculation is described based on the primary outcome requirements.
quotes: The sample size was estimated based on the systolic ambulatory BP response to exercise training and behavioral interventions. A sample of 184 subjects conferred adequate power to detect differences of 2.5 to 3.0 mmHg

analysis.item_name: Interim analyses and stopping guidelines (if any)
analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 16b (Interim analyses and stopping guidelines (if any)) reported?
analysis.comment: The manuscript describes early termination due to the COVID-19 pandemic in the 'Protocol Deviations' section, but does not describe any pre-planned interim analyses or stopping guidelines.

analysis.item_name: Random sequence generation: who generated and method used
analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 17a (Random sequence generation: who generated and method used) reported?
analysis.comment: The method of sequence generation is described as electronic.
quotes: The allocation sequence was generated electronically and maintained by a researcher not involved in interventions or data collection.

analysis.item_name: Type of randomisation and restriction details
analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 17b (Type of randomisation and restriction details) reported?
analysis.comment: The type of randomization (permuted blocks) and the allocation ratio (1:1) are specified.
quotes: Randomization followed a 1:1 ratio with permuted blocks of random sizes, allocating participants to combined exercise training or health education.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 18 (Allocation concealment mechanism) reported?
analysis.comment: The manuscript states that the sequence was maintained by a researcher independent of the interventions or data collection, which serves as the concealment mechanism.
quotes: The allocation sequence was generated electronically and maintained by a researcher not involved in interventions or data collection.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 19 (Randomisation implementation: access, enrolment, assignment) reported?
analysis.comment: The manuscript identifies that a researcher maintained the sequence, but it does not specify who enrolled the participants or who assigned them to the interventions.
quotes: The allocation sequence was generated electronically and maintained by a researcher not involved in interventions or data collection.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 20a (Blinding: who was blinded) reported?
analysis.comment: The manuscript identifies that outcome assessors were blinded.
quotes: Outcome assessors were blinded to participant allocation.

analysis.item_name: Blinding: how achieved and similarity of interventions (if blinded)
analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 20b (Blinding: how achieved and similarity of interventions (if blinded)) reported?
analysis.comment: The manuscript describes steps taken to minimize differences (attention control) between groups, specifically providing the exercise group with the health education content.
quotes: To minimize differences between the intervention groups, all the participants in the exercise group received summarized oral information related to all topics of the health education intervention at the end of each exercise session.

analysis.item_name: Statistical methods for primary/secondary outcomes and harms
analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 21a (Statistical methods for primary/secondary outcomes and harms) reported?
analysis.comment: Statistical methods including generalized estimated equations (GEE) and post-hoc Tukey tests are detailed.
quotes: Generalized estimated equations with an independence model as the covariance matrix and adjusted for baseline values were calculated for each variable of interest, with time and allocation group as factors.

analysis.item_name: Definition of analysis population and analysis group
analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 21b (Definition of analysis population and analysis group) reported?
analysis.comment: The intention-to-treat and per-protocol populations are clearly defined.
quotes: Datasets were divided in intention-to-treat (ITT) - where all randomized individuals with at least one measurement (irrespective of adherence and completion status) were considered - and per protocol - where only non-dropout and adherent ( ≥ 70%) subjects were considered.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 21c (Missing data handling) reported?
analysis.comment: The manuscript does not describe specific methods for handling missing data or the assumptions made regarding missingness, other than excluding individuals without at least one measurement from the ITT analysis.

analysis.item_name: Methods for additional analyses (subgroup/sensitivity) and prespecification
analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 21d (Methods for additional analyses (subgroup/sensitivity) and prespecification) reported?
analysis.comment: The manuscript describes the use of a prespecified per-protocol analysis as a sensitivity/additional analysis to the intention-to-treat analysis and provides the adherence threshold criteria.
quotes: Data analyses were conducted using the intention-to-treat (ITT) and a prespecified per-protocol approach.

Datasets were divided in intention-to-treat (ITT) - where all randomized individuals with at least one measurement (irrespective of adherence and completion status) were considered - and per protocol - where only non-dropout and adherent ( ≥ 70%) subjects were considered.

analysis.item_name: Participant flow numbers (assigned, received, analysed) and flow diagram
analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 22a (Participant flow numbers (assigned, received, analysed) and flow diagram) reported?
analysis.comment: The manuscript reports the number of participants enrolled, those who failed to finish, and the final number analyzed, while referencing a flow diagram.
quotes: A total of 168 participants were enrolled in the study. Eight participants were unable to finish the protocol. Therefore, data on 160 participants were analyzed. A flow diagram detailing the trial recruitment process is presented in Figure 1 .

analysis.item_name: Losses and exclusions after randomisation with reasons
analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 22b (Losses and exclusions after randomisation with reasons) reported?
analysis.comment: The manuscript provides the number of participants who did not complete the study but does not detail the specific reasons for these losses in the text.
quotes: Eight participants were unable to finish the protocol. Therefore, data on 160 participants were analyzed.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 23a (Recruitment and follow-up dates) reported?
analysis.comment: The recruitment dates are clearly stated in the text.
quotes: From September 2017 to June 2020, recruitment employed a multi-faceted approach across both centers, including electronic health records, word-of-mouth referrals, print and electronic flyers, and media outreach.

analysis.item_name: Why trial ended or was stopped (if relevant)
analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 23b (Why trial ended or was stopped (if relevant)) reported?
analysis.comment: The reasons for early termination and stopping recruitment are explicitly discussed.
quotes: Second, the trial was terminated early due to COVID-19 restrictions in June 2020. After consulting external advisors, the coordinating committee ended the trial with 168 participants randomized and 160 completed.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 24a (Intervention/comparator as actually administered (adherence/fidelity etc.)) reported?
analysis.comment: Adherence rates for both intervention groups are reported.
quotes: Adherence to the interventions was 52.5% for the combined exercise training group and 70.7% for the health education program in the intention-to-treat analysis.

analysis.item_name: Concomitant care received during the trial
analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 24b (Concomitant care received during the trial) reported?
analysis.comment: The authors state that concomitant pharmacological changes were not systematically monitored as a study limitation.
quotes: First, we did not systematically monitor changes in antihypertensive medication during the trial. Although we expect this was infrequent, this lack of specific control limit exploratory analysis of treatment responders.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 25 (Baseline characteristics table) reported?
analysis.comment: Baseline characteristics are described and referenced in Table 1.
quotes: The mean age for both groups was approximately 67 ± 5.5 years. The majority of the sample consisted of women, individuals identifying as white, and participants with a body mass index marginally within the obesity range ( Table 1 ).

analysis.item_name: Numbers analysed and outcome estimation for each outcome
analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 26 (Numbers analysed and outcome estimation for each outcome) reported?
analysis.comment: The manuscript reports the number of participants analyzed and provides effect estimates for the primary and secondary outcomes.
quotes: Therefore, data on 160 participants were analyzed.
Compared with health education, exercise training did not reduce 24-hour ambulatory systolic BP (129.1 vs 128.9 mmHg; P=0.98) or diastolic BP (74.2 vs 74.4 mmHg; P=0.90)

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 27 (Harms/unintended events in each group) reported?
analysis.comment: Adverse events are reported in a dedicated section.
quotes: In the exercise program group, a bone fracture was documented. This event occurred outside of the supervised exercise training sessions and, upon adjudication, was determined to be unrelated to the intervention protocol.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 28 (Ancillary (subgroup/sensitivity) analyses results) reported?
analysis.comment: The results of the prespecified per-protocol analysis are reported as a secondary analysis.
quotes: The per-protocol analyses, restricted to non-dropout participants with ≥ 70% adherence to interventions, yielded results consistent with those obtained using the ITT approach.

analysis.item_name: Interpretation balancing benefits/harms and other evidence
analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 29 (Interpretation balancing benefits/harms and other evidence) reported?
analysis.comment: The discussion interprets the results in the context of current evidence and balances the primary null findings with secondary benefits in functional capacity.
quotes: Contrary to our hypothesis, combined exercise training did not reduce ambulatory BP beyond the health education program. Although these findings differ from current evidence on exercise effectiveness for BP reduction, key methodological features distinguish the HAEL study from prior trials
Exercise training improved VO2peak and SPPB scores but not six-minute walk distance, highlighting potential cardiovascular and disability risk reductions regardless of BP effects.

analysis.item_question: Is CONSORT 2025 item 30 (Trial limitations (bias, imprecision, generalisability, multiplicity)) reported?
analysis.comment: The authors explicitly address several limitations including medication tracking (bias), early trial termination (imprecision), and the pragmatic nature of the study (generalisability).
quotes: First, we did not systematically monitor changes in antihypertensive medication during the trial... Second, the early termination of the study due to the COVID-19 pandemic may have affected the statistical power of our analyses.

A key strength is that the study was conducted in a low-resource, pragmatic setting that closely mimics real-life conditions... enhance both the rigor and generalizability of our findings to diverse healthcare settings.

guides.guideDescription: TIDieR is a 12-item intervention-reporting checklist and guide designed to improve completeness of intervention descriptions so studies can be interpreted, replicated, and implemented.

analysis.item_question: Is the intervention identified with a clear brief name or phrase?
analysis.comment: The interventions are clearly named as combined exercise training and a health education program.
quotes: To determine whether a pragmatic combined exercise training program is more effective than a health education program in reducing ambulatory BP in older adults with hypertension.

analysis.item_question: Is the rationale, theory, or goal of essential intervention elements described?
analysis.comment: The rationale for the pragmatic approach and specific exercise types (body weight/elastic bands) is described in the context of public health implementation.

analysis.item_question: Are physical/information materials used in intervention delivery or provider training described and locatable?

analysis.item_question: Are intervention schedule and dose details reported (frequency, period, number of sessions, duration, intensity/dose)?

quotes: Cardiorespiratory exercise intensity was based on a rate of perceived exertion of 14-15 on the Borg’s rate of perceived exertion scale 11 .

analysis.item_question: If intervention changed during study, are modifications described (what/why/when/how)?
analysis.comment: Modifications including center closure and early termination due to COVID-19 are described in the 'Protocol Deviations' section.
quotes: First, the Pelotas center closed due to low recruitment... Second, the trial was terminated early due to COVID-19 restrictions in June 2020.

analysis.item_question: If adherence/fidelity was assessed, are planned assessment methods, assessors, and maintenance strategies reported?
analysis.comment: The manuscript describes the use of a weekly survey to monitor activities and defines adherence thresholds (≥ 70%) for the per-protocol analysis.
quotes: All participants answered a weekly survey on pharmacological treatment changes, adverse events, and exercise performed outside of the study.

Datasets were divided in intention-to-treat (ITT) - where all randomized individuals with at least one measurement (irrespective of adherence and completion status) were considered - and per protocol - where only non-dropout and adherent ( ≥ 70%) subjects were considered.

analysis.item_question: If adherence/fidelity was assessed, is actual intervention delivery relative to plan reported?
analysis.comment: Actual adherence rates for both the exercise and health education groups are reported in the results section.
quotes: Adherence to the interventions was 52.5% for the combined exercise training group and 70.7% for the health education program in the intention-to-treat analysis.

guides.guideDescription: The ICMJE Recommendations provide a broad, cross-cutting framework for ethical conduct, transparent reporting, manuscript preparation, peer review, and publication practices in medical journals. They combine manuscript-level reporting expectations with requirements on authorship, conflicts of interest, participant protection, trial registration, data sharing, and responsible use of artificial intelligence.

analysis.item_name: Disclose relationships, activities, funding, and sponsor role transparently

quotes: Objective: To determine whether a pragmatic combined exercise training program is more effective than a health education program in reducing ambulatory BP in older adults with hypertension.

analysis.item_name: Describe participants, sex/gender, race/ethnicity, and measurement methods clearly
